DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.3.3
Checking the fastening of the feed motor
1. Turn the feed to a horizontal position.
2. Turn off the engine.
3. Check the tightness of the feed motor mounting bolts.
4. Tighten all loose bolts.
5. Visually check the condition of the feed motor and look for possible oil
leaks.
5.3.4
Checking the condition of the sprockets
1. Visually check the condition of the feed chain sprockets.
5.3.5
Checking the feed pins and their lockings
1. Check that all pins are properly in place.
2. Check that all lock rings, pins, and locking plates are in place.
3. Check the condition of the pins, lock rings, and locking plates.
4. Replace any worn pins and loose/damaged lock rings, pins, and locking
plates.
149 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.3.6
Checking the clearances of the carriages and the condition of the slide pieces
CRUSHING HAZARD!
Risk of death or serious injury.
Support the rotation head, hose reel, and pipe guide when replacing
the slide pieces.
The slide piece clearance is adjusted by means of the oval holes on the
carriage. If the pieces are so worn that the clearance (1) exceeds 3 mm,
the clearance must be adjusted to the specified value (1 mm). When
measuring the clearance, move the carriage such that the slide pieces on
the other side contact the beam (2).
1. Check the clearances of the rotation head carriage.
3
4
1
2
2. Check the clearances of the hose reel carriage.
3. Check the clearances of the pipe guide carriage.
4. If necessary, adjust the clearances by loosening the frame section
mounting screws (3) and locking screws (4) and by moving the frame
section closer to the slide beam. After adjustment, tighten the mounting
and locking screws. Apply threadlocker (Loctite 243 or similar) on the
locking screws. Note that there can be two overlapping locking screws.
5. Visually check the condition of the carriage slide pieces.

locking screws. Note that there can be two overlapping locking screws.
5. Visually check the condition of the carriage slide pieces.
6. After the adjustment, use the feed to run the rotation head between its
extreme positions a few times, and monitor the feed pressure while doing
this.
If the feed pressure clearly increases at some point of the rotation head's
movement range, determine the cause, and if necessary make the
clearances slightly wider.
150 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
Replace the slide pieces if their clearances cannot be adjusted to the
specified value or if the slide pieces are damaged.
5.3.7
Greasing the cylinder rod end bushings
1. Lubricate the rod end bearings of the feed extension cylinder through the
grease nipples.
2. Lubricate the rod end bearings of the moving suction head cylinder
through the grease nipples.
151 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.3.8
Greasing the sprocket and tightening screw
1. Grease the sprocket and tightening screw through the grease nipples.
5.3.9
Greasing the pipe guide
1. Lubricate the pipe guide greasing points on both sides.
5.3.10
Greasing the hose reel bearings
1. Grease the hose reel bearings.
152 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.3.11
Checking the tightness of the hose reel hoses
The tightness of the hoses should be checked when the feed is in the
tramming position and the rotation head is in its extreme position, either at
the rear or front.
1. Check the tightness of the hose reel hoses.
The hose slack should be 30–60 mm.
2. If necessary, adjust the tightness of the hoses using the lead-through
connectors. After this, tighten all hoses to correct tightness by moving the
lead-through plate.

Pipe handler
5.4.1
Checking the pipe handler pins and roll pins
1. Check that all pins are properly in place.
2. Check that all roll pins are in place.
3. Check the condition of the pins and roll pins.
4. Replace any worn pins and also any loose or damaged roll pins.
153 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.4.2
Checking the condition and fastening of the pipe handler
1. Check the tightness of the pipe handler mounting bolts.
2. Tighten all loose bolts.
3. Visually check the condition of the pipe handler.
5.4.3
Checking the pipe handler jaws
1. Check the condition of the jaw piece roll pins.
2. Replace damaged roll pins.
3. Check the condition of the jaw pieces.
4. Replace worn or damaged jaw pieces.
154 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.4.4
Checking the fastening and condition of the break out table jaw pieces
1. Check the condition and fastening of the break out table jaw pieces (8
pcs.).
2. Tighten all loose mounting screws.
3. Replace worn or otherwise damaged jaw pieces.
155 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.4.5
Lubricating the pipe handler greasing points
1. Lubricate all pipe handler greasing points: cylinder rod end bearings,
pins, and shafts.

Pipe winch (optional)
5.5.1
Changing the grease in the pipe winch gear
1. Change the grease in the pipe winch gear.
156 (348)

Hydraulic rotation head HTRH6
5.6.1
Checking the installation of the floating spindle
When taking a new rotation head into use or if the floating spindle has been
removed, check the tightness of the thread after one hour of drilling.
1. Check that the end faces of the thread are attached to each other.
2. Retighten the floating spindle if there is a gap between the end faces.
5.6.2
Checking the condition of the shaft seals
1. Check the shaft seals for possible leaks.
A seal is leaking if there is a noticeable air flow from:
• a pressure relief valve
• under the seal lip of the front seal
• under the seal lip of the floating spindle's shaft seal.
2. Replace any leaking seals.
157 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.6.3
Greasing the rotation head
Note!
The floating spindle and the flushing head are greased with different
types of grease. Refer to the section 'Lubricants and capacities' for
the correct grease types.
1. Grease the flushing head from the grease nipples (4 pcs).
Every grease nipple delivers grease to a different section of the rotation
head, therefore it is important to apply grease through every grease
nipple.
Apply grease until fresh grease starts to come out.
2. Grease the floating spindle from the grease nipples (2 pcs).
Apply grease the amount of ten strikes of the standard grease press.
158 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.6.4
Checking the fastening of the rotation head
Note!
Only use a calibrated, high-quality torque wrench.
1. Check the tightness of the mounting bolts (18 pcs) to slide piece frames.
2. Tighten any loose bolts.
The correct tightening torque is 120 Nm.
5.6.5
Checking the tightness of the floating spindle bolts
Note!
Only use a calibrated, high-quality torque wrench.
1. Check the tightness of the bolts (12 pcs).
The correct tightening torque is 130 Nm.
159 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.6.6
Checking the planetary gearbox oil level
A
B
1. Move the rotation head to a horizontal or vertical position.
2. Remove the filling plug.
3. Check the oil level:
• In the horizontal position, the oil level (A) should be 30 mm below the
sealing surface of the filling plug.
• In the vertical position, the oil level (B) should be at the level of the
threaded plug hole.
4. Add oil if needed.
5. Close the filling plug.
160 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.6.7
Changing the planetary gearbox oil
Change the oil directly after operating the rotation head, while the oil is still
warm.
1. Move the rotation head to a horizontal position so that the oil filler plug
(1) points straight upwards.
2. Place a receptacle under the planetary gearbox oil drain plug (2).
1
2
3. Open the oil filler plug (1) and oil drain plug (2), and let the used oil drain
off into the receptacle.
4. Clean the plugs and the sealing surfaces.
5. Close the oil drain plug (2).
6. Add new oil into the planetary gearbox.
7. Close the oil filler plug (1).
161 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.6.8
Checking the rotation head oil levels
1
2
1. Move the rotation head to a horizontal position so that the oil filling plugs
(1) and (2) point straight upwards.
2. Clean the area around the filling plugs.
3. Remove the filling plug (1) and check the oil level of the driveline
chamber.
4. Add oil into the driveline chamber if needed.
5. Close the filling plug (1).
6. Remove the filling plug (2) and check the oil level of the bearing
chamber.
7. Add oil into the bearing chamber if needed.
8. Close the filling plug (2).
162 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.6.9
Changing the rotation head oils
Change the oils directly after operating the rotation head, while the oils are
still warm.

(1) and (2) point straight upwards.
2. Clean the area around the filling and draining plugs.
3. Place a receptacle under the draining plugs (3) and (4).
4. Open the filling plugs (1) and (2).

Carrier
5.7.1
Washing the rig
1. Wash the rig regularly.
Do not use solvents, which could damage the rig’s rubber or plastic parts.
5.7.2
Checking the welds
1. Check all carrier welds regularly.
Do not use the drill rig if there are fractures or other faults in the welds.
2. Repair faulty welded seams before using the drill rig.
If necessary, contact Sandvik Service.
170 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.7.3
Checking the condition of the fire extinguishers
1. Check the condition of the fire extinguishers.
2. If necessary, clean the extinguisher and ensure that it includes the
operating instructions.
3. Make sure that the extinguisher's indicator gauge is not in the red zone. If
the indicator is in the red zone, the fire extinguisher must be serviced
immediately at an authorised service shop.
When a fire extinguisher is being serviced, replace it with a
corresponding extinguisher.
Do not use the drilling rig without appropriate safety equipment.
5.7.4
Checking the fastening of the track plates
1. Check the fastening of track plates visually.
2. Tighten all loose mounting screws and replace missing mounting screws
with new ones.

Cleaning the diesel engine
1. Clean the diesel engine.
When cleaning the engine, prevent water from damaging electric parts of
the engine such as the alternator, starter motor, and ECM. Do not use
any solvents for cleaning that might damage the plastic or rubber parts of
the engine.
2. Remove any impurities and splashes from the engine surface.
Accumulated grease or oil on the engine is a fire hazard.

The air filter prevents dust and other impurities from entering the engine.
Clean intake air is essential for the correct operation of the engine. Any
impurities in the intake air cause the engine to wear rapidly. It is therefore
important to check and perform maintenance of the intake system regularly
and correctly.

5.8.9
Checking the engine grounding
1. Check the fastening of the grounding cable between the frame and the
engine at every oil change.
The grounding cable between the frame and the engine is connected to
the frame's grounding point and under the starter motor's mounting
screw.
2. Also check the grounding stud of the engine control unit.
All grounding connections must be tightened. If there is corrosion on the
connections, clean the connections.

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.8.10
Cleaning the radiator core
1. Turn off the engine.
2. Check that the radiator has no damaged fins, corrosion, or dirt (grease,
oil, insects, leaves, etc.).
3. The radiator can be cleaned with a high-pressure washer or with
compressed air:
• When cleaning the radiator with a high-pressure washer, keep the
nozzle at a safe distance and do not use too high pressure to avoid
damaging the radiator.
• When cleaning the radiator with compressed air, do not use a
pressure of above 4 bar.
4. Check the cleanliness of the radiator core by shining a flashlight through
the core from the opposite side.
If you cannot see any light through the core, there is still dirt in the core
and it must be cleaned more thoroughly.
189 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.8.11
Cleaning the intercooler core
1. Turn off the engine.
2. Check that the intercooler has no damaged fins, corrosion, or foreign
matter (dirt, grease, oil, insects, leaves, etc.).
3. You can clean the intercooler in various ways:
• Clean the intercooler with a high-pressure washer. Keep the nozzle
at a safe distance and do not use too high pressure, to avoid
damaging the intercooler.
• Clean the intercooler with compressed air. Do not use a pressure of
above 4 bar.
4. Check the cleanliness of the intercooler core by shining a flashlight
through the core from the opposite side.
If you cannot see any light through the core, there is still dirt in the core
and it must be cleaned more thoroughly.

Cabin
5.9.1
Cleaning the control devices
1. Clean the armrest panels and auxiliary panels.
2. Clean the display and gauges.
3. Clean the pedals.
5.9.2
Checking the operation of the cabin door switch
1. Start the rotation clockwise.
2. Open the door.
• If rotation stops, the door switch is OK.
• If rotation does not stop, the door switch is defective and must be
replaced.
5.9.3
Checking the operation of the armrest limit switches
1. Start the rotation clockwise.
2. Lift the left armrest up.
• If rotation stops, the limit switch is OK.
• If rotation does not stop, the limit switch is defective and must be
replaced.
3. Lower the left armrest.
4. Restart the rotation clockwise.
5. Lift the right armrest up.
• If rotation stops, the limit switch is OK.
• If rotation does not stop, the limit switch is defective and must be
replaced.
6. Lower the right armrest.

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.9.4
Replacing the cabin air filters
1. Open the air filter housing cover.
2. Remove the old air filters.
3. Clean the air filter housing.
4. Install the new filters in place.
5. Check the condition of the cover seals.
Replace the seal if necessary.
6. Close the air filter housing cover.

Hydraulic system
5.10.1
Cleaning the radiator core
1. Turn off the engine.
2. Check that the radiator has no damaged fins, corrosion, or dirt (grease,
oil, insects, leaves, etc.).
3. The radiator can be cleaned with a high-pressure washer or with
compressed air:
• When cleaning the radiator with a high-pressure washer, keep the
nozzle at a safe distance and do not use too high pressure to avoid
damaging the radiator.
• When cleaning the radiator with compressed air, do not use a
pressure of above 4 bar.
4. Check the cleanliness of the radiator core by using a flashlight to point
light through the opposite side of the core.
If you cannot see any light through the core, there is still dirt in the core
and it must be cleaned more thoroughly.

2. Ensure that the connector and cable are intact and properly positioned.
5.10.3
Checking the temperature sensor
The hydraulic oil tank temperature sensor must be checked when the tank
has been drained for an oil change or for cleaning.
1. Visually check the condition of the temperature sensor, cable, and
connector.
2. Warm the sensor and measure its temperature.
3. Compare the measured temperature value with the temperature
displayed by the control system.
4. Replace the temperature sensor if necessary.

Replacing the hydraulic oil pressure filter
1. Turn off the engine.
2. Ensure that the hydraulic system is not pressurized.
3. Open the filter housing, and remove the used filter cartridge and seal.
4. Clean the filter housing and sealing surfaces.
5. Install the new filter cartridge and seal in their place.
6. Close the filter housing by twisting.
7. Ensure the tightness of the filter housing upon the next start-up.

Replacing the hydraulic oil return filter

2. Open the filter cover.
3. Lift the used filter out of the tank.
4. Install the new filter carefully.
5. Ensure that the cover seal is intact. Replace the seal with a new one if
necessary.
6. Reinstall the cover.

Checking the operation of the hydraulic oil level switch

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.11.5
Changing the compressor oil
The compressor's oil-change interval depends on the oil type used and on
the operating conditions. For more information, refer to the compressor's oil
recommendations.
Before changing the oil, ensure that there is no pressure in the pneumatic
system.
1. Remove the protective plate under the tank from the drilling unit bottom.
2. Drain the oil from the tank into an oil receptacle by opening the drain
cock on the tank bottom.
3. Drain the oil from the lubrication block into an oil receptacle by opening
the drain cock located below the lubrication block.
4. Close the drain cocks.
5. Reinstall the protective plate on the drilling unit bottom.
6. Add compressor oil through the filler opening on the side of the tank.
For the correct oil grade and volume, refer to the “Lubricant and coolant
recommendations” section.
7. Check the sight glass on the tank to ensure that the oil level is correct.
The oil level must be between the Min and Max marks.
215 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.11.6
Replacing the compressor oil filter
1. Ensure that there is no pressure in the pneumatic system.
2. Open the bleeding screw on the filter housing cover about one turn to
release any pressure from the filter housing.
3. Drain the oil from the lubrication block into an oil receptacle by opening
the drain cock located below the lubrication block.
4. Close the bleeding screw.
5. Open the filter housing cover.
6. Remove the old filter and seal.
7. Clean the filter housing and sealing surfaces.
8. Fit the new filter and seal in place.
9. Fit the filter housing in place and tighten the mounting screws.
5.11.7
Replacing the compressor blow-out breather
1. Ensure that there is no pressure in the pneumatic system.
2. Open the four Allen screws (8-mm head) and lower the filter bowl down.
NOTE: Do not misplace the O-ring.
3. Remove the filter bracket from the bottom of the bowl and clean the bowl
of any impurities. Place the bracket back in the bottom of the bowl.
4. Remove the filter by pulling down and replace with a new filter.
5. Make sure that the filter bowl O-ring is intact and in the correct place.
6. Place the bowl back and tighten the screws.

5.11.8
Replacing the oil separator filter cartridge
When replacement of the oil separator cartridge coincides with the same
hour count as cleaning of the compressor oil receiver, replace the filter
cartridge at the same time as cleaning the receiver.
1. Ensure that there is no pressure in the pneumatic system.
2. Place the valve (2) onto a support and detach it from the flange (3).
3. Open the receiver cover (1) and disconnect the grounding strips from the
filter cartridge (4).
4. Remove the old filter cartridge and seal.
5. Fit the new filter cartridge and seal in place.
6. Reconnect the grounding strips to the filter cartridge.
7. Reinstall the receiver cover and tighten the cover mounting screws.
The correct tightening torque for the cover screws is 170 Nm.
8. Reinstall the valve (2).

5.11.10
Checking the operation of the compressor oil thermostat
If the compressor oil temperature increases too much or is too low, clean
the thermostat and verify its correct operation.
1. Ensure that there is no pressure in the pneumatic system.
2. Place the collection receptacle under the thermostat.
218 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
3. Remove the thermostat and screw in the plug in place of the thermostat
in the radiator.
4. Clean the thermostat.
5. Immerse the thermostat in clean compressor oil and warm the oil. Do not
use open flames to warm the oil.
6. Measure the oil temperature constantly and monitor the thermostat
during warming.
7. The thermostat should operate at approximately 60 °C.
• If the thermostat operates at the correct temperature, reinstall it.
• If the thermostat operates at too low or high temperature or does not
operate at all, replace the thermostat with a new one.
5.11.11
Cleaning the radiator core
1. Turn off the engine.
2. Check that the radiator has no damaged fins, corrosion, or dirt (grease,
oil, insects, leaves, etc.).
3. The radiator can be cleaned with a high-pressure washer or with
compressed air:
• When cleaning the radiator with a high-pressure washer, keep the
nozzle at a safe distance and do not use too high pressure to avoid
damaging the radiator.
• When cleaning the radiator with compressed air, do not use a
pressure of above 4 bar.
219 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
4. Check the cleanliness of the radiator core by using a flashlight to point
light through the opposite side of the core.
If you cannot see any light through the core, there is still dirt in the core
and it must be cleaned more thoroughly.
5.11.12
Checking the condition and fastening of hose restraints
WARNING! DANGER OF PROJECTILE INJURY!
Disconnection or breakage of a pressurized hose can cause serious
injury or death.
Do not use the drill rig if not all hose restraints are in place. Do not
use the drill rig if even one of the hose restraints is damaged.
1. Check the condition of the hose restraint cables.
2. Check the fastening of the hose restraints. Tighten the mounting screws
if necessary.
3. Replace any defective parts of the hose restraints before the next use of
the drill rig.
220 (348)

DTH hammer lubrication device
5.12.1
Draining the condensed water from the oil tank
1. Empty the condensed water into a receptacle using the drain valve.
5.12.2
Replacing the oil tank breather
1. Replace the breather at the intervals specified in the maintenance
instructions or as required, e.g. in the event that the breather has
become wet.
5.12.3
Cleaning the oil receiver
1. Empty the oil receiver into the receptacle using the drain cock.
2. Remove the breather.
3. Wash the inside of the receiver.
4. Dry the receiver.
5. Close the drain cock.
6. Fill the receiver with oil.
7. Re-install the breather.
221 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.12.4
Checking the operation of the monitoring unit
CT5
CT1
1. Turn the flushing on.
2. Check the operation of the monitoring unit as follows:
• Disconnect the directional control valve (CT5) plug. The control
system display should show an alarm.
• Disconnect the flow monitoring cable from the monitoring unit (CT1).
The control system display should show an alarm after four
operational cycles.
• Short-circuit the flow monitoring cable to the pump body. The control
system display should show an alarm.
222 (348)

Fuel system
5.13.1
Draining water and deposits from the fuel tank
Regularly drain water from the fuel tank.
1. Place a fuel receptacle under the drain hose.
2. Drain water and any deposits by opening the valve, closing it only when
clean fuel runs from the tank.
3. Close the valve.
When the ambient temperature is below zero, water must be drained after
using the rig before the water freezes.
223 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.13.2
Replacing the fuel water separator filter
Replace the filter at the intervals specified in the maintenance instructions
or if power loss is observed. Impurities gradually clog the filter element,
causing restricted fuel flow to the engine and power loss and/or problems
with starting.
Note!
Do not fill the filter with fuel before installation.
1. Close the valves on the fuel supply line between the fuel tank and diesel
engine.
CAT C9.3
CAT C13
2. Drain some fuel from the bowl into a receptacle by opening the drain
cock.
224 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
3. Unscrew the filter element and bowl together from the filter base.
4. Empty the fuel from the bowl into the receptacle.
5. Unscrew the bowl from the element and replace the O-ring.
6. Apply clean fuel or engine oil to the sealing surfaces of the new element
and O-ring.
7. Reattach the bowl to the element and then screw the element and bowl
back onto the filter base by hand.
Do not use tools for the tightening.
8. Open the valves on the fuel supply line between the fuel tank and diesel
engine.
225 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.13.3
Replacing the fuel filter
Replace the filters (2 pcs) at the intervals specified in the maintenance
instructions or if power loss is observed.
Note!
Do not fill the filters with fuel before installation.
CAT C9.3
226 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
CAT C13
1. Close the valve on the fuel supply line between the fuel tank and diesel
engine.
2. Place a fuel receptacle under the filter.
3. Clean the filter and the area around the filter.
4. Remove the filter element by turning it counterclockwise.
5. Clean the filter-base sealing surfaces.
6. Apply clean fuel to the sealing surfaces of the filter base and new filter.
7. Screw the new filter element in place manually.
Do not use tools for tightening.
8. Open the valve on the fuel supply line between the fuel tank and diesel
engine.
227 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.13.4
Filling the fuel system
The manual switch is located on primary filter base/electric filling pump
assembly.
1. Hold the manual switch in the up position until fuel has filled the water
separator.
CAT C9.3
CAT C13
2. Continue to hold the switch for 30 seconds after the water separator is
full.
3. Attempt to start the engine. If engine starts and runs rough or misfires,
operate at low idle until the engine is running smoothly. If the engine
cannot be started, continue to fill the fuel system for 30 more seconds.
228 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.13.5
Replacing the fuel tank breather
The fuel tank breather must be replaced earlier than specified if fuel has
made it wet.
1. Turn off the engine.
2. Remove the old breather by turning it counterclockwise.
3. Install the new breather by putting it in place and turning it clockwise.
229 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.13.6
Cleaning the fuel tank
Take the large size of the fuel tank into consideration when selecting a
receptacle for the fuel. Run the fuel tank as low as possible before stopping
the engine. However, do not run the engine so long that the fuel flow stops
altogether.
1. Turn off the engine.
2. Empty the fuel tank into a receptacle using the drain cock.
3. Open the tank hatch.
4. Clean, rinse, and dry the tank thoroughly.
5. Close the drain cock.
6. Reinstall the tank hatch. Use a new seal if necessary.
7. Ensure that the tank is clean before filling it.
230 (348)

Diesel exhaust fluid (DEF) system
5.14.1
Replacing the DEF/AdBlue pump filter assembly
In TIER4 Final engines equipped with Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)
system, the Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF/AdBlue ® ) pump filter kit and filter
assembly need to be replaced every 5000 engine hours.
1. Open the DEF/AdBlue ® filter assembly.
2. Replace the DEF/AdBlue ® filter kit.
3. Close the DEF/AdBlue ® filter assembly.
5.14.2
Changing the DEF injector
Change the DEF injector.
For more information, refer to the engine manufacturer’s maintenance
instructions.
231 (348)

Dust collector system
5.15.1
Checking the condition of suction hoses and connections
1. Check that there are no leaks in the dust collector suction line hoses and
connections.
2. Reshape any depressed suction hoses so that they are round.
3. Replace any damaged hoses.
232 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.15.2
Checking the condition of the filter elements
Dust emission from the dust-collector air outlet indicates a damaged filter
element. Replace a damaged element immediately, since the passage of
dust through the damaged element causes wearing of the dust-collector
impeller.
3
5

2

3

4

5
Filter cover
1. Ensure that the dust collector impeller has stopped.
It takes a while for the impeller to stop after the engine is shut down.
2. Open the dust collector hatch.
3. Open the filter's tightening latch using the twisting tool.
4. Remove the filter tightener from the filter cover.
5. Lower the filter element down.
6. Remove the rest of the filter elements in the same manner.
The filter cover comes off by twisting it counterclockwise. You may need
to remove one or several filter covers to gain better working access. If
necessary, use the support tool to help support the rear filter elements
and covers when removing them.
7. Check the condition of the filter elements.
Clean the elements with compressed air if necessary.
233 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
8. If any element is in bad condition, replace it.
9. Re-install the filter elements in reverse order.
10. Make sure that all filter tightener latches are in their upper position.
11. Close the dust collector hatch.
Note!
During cleaning, the compressed air nozzle must be kept at a safe
distance from the filter element to prevent it from becoming
damaged.
5.15.3
Checking the operation of pulse jet cleaning
Blow-out valves are found on the dust collector pulse jet cleaning line. The
blow-out valves operate in turn, blowing compressed air into the filter
elements. The cleaning function can be checked by monitoring the blow-out
valve operation. Normally, the LED on the blow-out valve is lit when the
valve is activated. When the blow-out valve blows compressed air into the
filters, a blowing sound is heard.
234 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.15.4
Checking the integrity of the dust collector
When the integrity of the dust collector housing is sufficient, the dust
connector does not suck in particles through the cracks on the maintenance
shaft nor from other inappropriate places.
1. Check the integrity of the dust collector.
2. Replace the seals if necessary.
5.15.5
Checking the condition of the dust collector's impact plate
1. Check the condition of the steel grate at the dust collector intake
opening. If there is a hole in the grate, replace the grate.
235 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.15.6
Checking the condition of the pre-cleaner skirt rubber
A damaged pre-cleaner skirt rubber reduces the effectiveness of the dust
collector system.
1. Check the condition of the skirt rubber at the opening of the pre-cleaner.
2. If the rubber is worn or otherwise damaged, replace it.
5.15.7
Checking the condition of the pre-cleaner's rubber lining
1. Turn off the engine.
2. Wait for the dust collector impeller to stop.
3. Open the pre-cleaner cover.
4. Check the condition of the rubber lining inside the pre-cleaner.
5. If there is a hole in the rubber lining or if it has become loose, replace it
with a new rubber lining.
236 (348)

Do not place hands or other body parts under any part of the feed

Do not place hands or other body parts under the drill bit, hammer,

1. Turn off the engine.
2. Wait for the dust collector impeller to stop.
3. Check the condition of the skirt rubber at the opening of the suction head.
4. Check the condition of the seal rubbers in the suction head.
5. Replace any damaged rubbers.
5.15.9
Moving suction head cylinder lubrication
1. Lubricate the moving suction head cylinder greasing points.

Main electrical system
5.16.1
Checking the electrolyte level and battery cable connections

DANGER
RISK OF EXPLOSION!
Risk of death or serious injury.
Do not expose batteries to open fire. If you need extra light to check
the batteries, use a flashlight or similar.
1. Turn off the engine.
2. Turn the electrical system main switch to position 0.
3. Check the battery electrolyte level. The electrolyte level in the cells must
be above the plates.
4. Add distilled water, if necessary.
5. Check the fastening of the battery cables.
6. Tighten the cable connections and cable shoes if necessary.
238 (348)

text:
DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.16.2
Cleaning the battery and the battery terminals
1. Turn off the engine.
2. Turn the electrical system main switch to position 0.
3. Clean the batteries.
4. Remove any oxidation from the battery terminals and connectors.
5. Grease the battery terminals and connectors with battery terminal grease
to prevent oxidation.
5.16.3
Checking the condition of electric cables and connectors
1. Check the condition of electric cables.
2. Check the condition and fastening of electric connectors.
Replace any faulty cables or connectors. If necessary, contact Sandvik
Service.
5.16.4
Checking electric cabinets and boxes
1. Check that the electric cabinet doors and box covers are closed securely.
Also check the tightness of doors and covers.
2. Check the fastening of electric cabinet and box connectors.
3. Check the integrity and tightness of sealing bushings on the electric
cabinets and boxes.
4. Check that possible water holes are not clogged.
Repair any faults and deficiencies. If necessary, contact Sandvik Service.
239 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
5.16.5
Checking the fastening of electric cables
1. Check the fastening of electric cables.
2. Check that cables do not chafe on anything.
3. Check that the cables have not chafed or pulled free of their fastenings.
Firmly attach any loose cables. Repair other faults and deficiencies. If
necessary, contact Sandvik Service.

Control system
5.17.1
Checking the rotation-head position sensor calibration
1. Detach the pipe/pipes from the rotation head and move them to the
cassette.
2. Run the rotation head to its extreme position against the front endstop.
3. Run the rotation head to its extreme position against the rear endstop.
The control system will check the accuracy of the calibration and, if
necessary, correct possible errors.

Central lubrication system (optional)
5.18.1
Checking the pipes and hoses
1. Check the lubrication pipes and hoses in locations where they are
vulnerable to external damage.
2. Replace the damaged components.
5.18.2
Cleaning the filling connector filter
1. Clean the filling connector filter.
240 (348)

General
WARNING! HEALTH AND ENVIRONMENTAL HAZARD!
Skin contact and inhalation of vapours when handling fluids could
cause damage to human health.
Incorrect disposal of fluids and greases could cause damage to the
environment.
Use personal protective equipment when handling fluids and
greases, and dispose of all fluids and greases in accordance with
local regulations.
Choosing the oil according to its properties
The following issues must be considered:
• viscosity
• stability of properties throughout the oil’s service life
• water separation ability
• anti-oxidation and anti-foaming properties
• health effects
• environmental effects
Note!
Check the original oil type from the machine card or the maintenance
instructions.
Choosing the oil according to the application
The following issues must be considered:
• Sandvik's oil recommendation
• the component manufacturer's oil recommendation (e.g. diesel engine,
transmission)
• environmental/site-specific/safety requirements
- biodegradable oils
- less flammable fluids
Mistakes in choosing and using oil
Components may be damaged if
• a wrong oil type has been chosen
• a wrong oil viscosity has been chosen

Mistakes in choosing and using oil
Components may be damaged if
• a wrong oil type has been chosen
• a wrong oil viscosity has been chosen
• the recommended oil change interval is exceeded.
Oils must be changed at certain intervals because the properties of the oil
and its additives deteriorate and are lost as the oil ages.
Note!
Observe the color and clearness of the oil. Water and other
impurities in the oil can cause malfunctions in the hydraulic system
and serious component damage (e.g. to rock drills). If the oil is light
grey or dull, change the oil. Find the cause of the oil impurity.
242 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
Oil viscosity
Too low viscosity causes:
• breaking of the lubrication film between contact surfaces. The resulting
metal-to-metal contact between surfaces causes rapid wear and
increases the need for maintenance.
• internal leaks in components, which reduces efficiency.
Too high viscosity causes:
• flow losses in the system, which also reduces efficiency
• increase in the load on the seals due to increased return line pressure
• for certain rock drills, wear of the rotation shaft and thrust bearing due to
increased return line pressure
• too thick oil increases the risk of cavitation in pumps.
SAE classification (engine oils)
(SAE = Society of Automotive Engineers)
Viscosity indicates the oil's ability to flow. Viscosity is measured at a high
and low temperature and is indicated as an SAE grade, e.g. SAE 40. For
multigrade oils, e.g. 5W-40, the first value (5W) indicates the oil’s viscosity
at low temperatures, and the second value (40) indicates the oil’s viscosity
when it is hot.
ISO classification (industrial oils)
(ISO = International Organization for Standardization)
The viscosity of industrial oils is determined by the ISO-VG standard (ISO

(ISO = International Organization for Standardization)
The viscosity of industrial oils is determined by the ISO-VG standard (ISO
3448). The ISO grade number indicates the oil’s viscosity at +40°C,
expressed in centistokes (cSt, kinematic viscosity). For example, oil graded
as ISO-VG 68 has a viscosity of 68 cSt at +40°C.
Choosing the fuel
Fuel is chosen according to the properties of the fuel and the application.
When choosing the fuel, at least the following issues must be considered:
• Sandvik's fuel recommendation
• the engine manufacturer's fuel recommendation
• environmental/site-specific/safety requirements
• fuel sulfur content
243 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
Sulfur content for US EPA Tier 4 Interim/Final and EU Stage IIIB/IV
engines
US EPA Tier 4 Interim/Final and EU Stage IIIB/IV engines require ultra low
sulfur diesel fuel (ULSD) to be emissions compliant.
RISK OF ENGINE DAMAGE!
Use ONLY ultra low sulfur diesel (ULSD) fuel with a maximum of
0.0015% (15 mg/kg) sulfur content.
Failure to do so can permanently damage engine and after-treatment
systems within a short period of time.

NOTICE
Note! Diesel fuel quality and fuel sulfur content must comply with all
existing emissions regulations for the area in which the engine
operates.
Compressor oil and refrigerant for the air conditioning unit
Refer to the manual for the air conditioning unit.

Hydraulic system
Oil viscosity recommendations are based on the operating temperature of
the oil.
Note!
Never mix different types of hydraulic system oils. Mixing can
damage the hydraulic components.
The selected hydraulic oil must be high quality and must retain its
properties in fluctuating temperature conditions. Furthermore, it should
contain additives typical of high performance hydraulic oils.
When choosing the viscosity of the oil to be used, the temperature of the
drilling environment is important. Pay attention to temperature fluctuation.
When choosing the oil, consider the following:
1. Check or estimate the operating temperature of the oil.
2. The viscosity of oils at their operating temperature should be close to 60
cSt, and any variation in continuous use should remain within the range
50–110 cSt.
The following variation limits are permitted temporarily (but not
constantly):
• 110–200 cSt as a result of a low temperature peak, or
• 30–50 cSt as a result of a high temperature peak.
The values indicated in the tables should be considered first and
foremost. In each case you must always make sure that the product in
question has the required properties.

foremost. In each case you must always make sure that the product in
question has the required properties.
3. If the drilling environment temperature does not vary greatly, we
recommend that you use single-grade oils which undergo minimal
viscosity variation during use.
244 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
4. If the drilling conditions are such that single-grade oils do not remain
within the given viscosity limits, choose a multigrade oil.
5. The oil should be changed once a year unless heavy use requires more
frequent changes. This change interval recommendation is based on the
extent that the oil ages and gathers moisture over a one-year period.
For arctic conditions (temperatures below –20°C), we recommend synthetic
oils.
Maximum permissible degree of contamination of the hydraulic fluid;
cleanliness class 20/15/12 according to the ISO 4406 (c).
A recommended water content for the hydraulic oils is <1000 ppm.

Biodegradable hydraulic oils
Sandvik sets the same high technical quality requirements for
biodegradable oils as are set for conventional mineral oils. In addition, the
oils must be environmentally friendly in terms of the following quality
requirements:
• biodegradability
• toxicity
• use and disposal.
The international standard ISO 15380 specifies the requirements for
environmentally acceptable hydraulic fluids.

text:
DI450-DI560 TIER4
Fire retardant hydraulic fluids
Sandvik sets the same high technical quality requirements for less
flammable hydraulic fluids as are set for conventional mineral oils. In
addition, the oils must be non-flammable, with self-extinguishing properties.
A range of fire retardant fluids are available on the market, the properties of
which differ greatly.
In risk-prone conditions, Sandvik recommends the use of fluids that comply
with the ISO 6743-4 HFD standard for synthetic fluids.
Note!
For safety, performance and environmental reasons, Sandvik does
not allow mixing of different hydraulic oils/fluids.
These fluids must be compatible with the majority of metals and seal
materials.
Sandvik uses fire retardant and biodegradable HFD fluids as factory-fill oils:
Fuchs Plantoflux 68-AT-S (Tampere plant) and Condat D68 (Lyon plant).
Sandvik recommends these fluids for use in drilling rigs. Since the
availability of these fluids can be limited in certain market areas, Sandvik
also approves the following alternative:
• Quintolubric 888-68 (Quaker Chemicals)
Sandvik factory fill oils are totally miscible with the fluid mentioned above.

• Quintolubric 888-68 (Quaker Chemicals)
Sandvik factory fill oils are totally miscible with the fluid mentioned above.
Thus, the factory filled oil can be changed as necessary in connection with
the rig start-up inspection without flushing the hydraulic system. Sandvik
does not approve continuous mixing of hydraulic fluids.
Note!
The guarantee is valid only if the recommended fluids are used.
Oils and fluids suitable for hydraulic systems are, e.g.
• Fuchs Plantoflux 68-AT-S (less flammable hydraulic fluid)

text:
Diesel engine
API engine oil classification
The API (American Petroleum Institute) diesel engine oil classification has
two-letter designations, the first letter being “C”. The current classification
designations for four-stroke diesel engines are API CG-4, API CH-4, API
CI-4 ja API CJ-4. The higher the second letter in the alphabet, the higher
the oil quality.
Fuel sulphur content, Caterpillar (Tier 4)

Ambient temperature and atmospheric humidity are the most important

text:
Transmission and driveline oils
API quality classification
The API (American Petroleum Institute) transmission oil recommendations
are API GL-1...API GL-5. The higher the last digit, the higher the EP
additive level is.
For example, API-GL-5 is an EP oil with a high additive level, suitable for
hypoid gears.
ISO 12925-1 quality classification
ISO (International Organization for Standardization) industrial transmission
oil recommendations are ISO 12925-1 type CKB... ISO 12925-1 type CKD.
The higher the third letter, the more additives there are in the oil.
For example, ISO 12925-1 type CKD. Lubricants with oxidation resistance,
anti-corrosion, anti-foam, pressure resistance, and anti-wear properties.
Improved thermal/oxidation resistance properties enable use at high
temperatures.
SAE viscosity classification (transmission)
SAE (Society of Automotive Engineers) Viscosity indicates the oil's ability to
flow. Viscosity is measured at a high and low temperature and is indicated
as an SAE grade, e.g. SAE 90. For multigrade oils, e.g. 75W-90, the first
value 75W indicates the viscosity at low temperatures, and the second
value 90 indicates the oil viscosity when it is hot.

value 75W indicates the viscosity at low temperatures, and the second
value 90 indicates the oil viscosity when it is hot.
ISO viscosity classification (industrial oils)
( ISO = International Organization for Standardization) The viscosity of
industrial lubricants is determined by the ISO-VG standard (ISO 3448). The
ISO grade number indicates the oil viscosity at +40 °C, expressed in
centistokes (cSt, kinematic viscosity). For example, oil graded as ISO-VG
150 has a viscosity of 150 cSt at + 40 °C.

text:
Engine coolant
Caterpillar engines
Engine coolant is a mixture of water and corrosion prevention agents / anti-
freeze agent. To prevent corrosion and to elevate the boiling point of the
coolant, the mixture must be used in the cooling system throughout the
year.
When choosing the corrosion preventing agents / anti-freeze agent for the
engine coolant, refer to the engine manufacturer's manual.
Engine coolant must be changed at certain intervals. The characteristics of
the coolant and its additives weaken and are lost as the coolant ages.
Refer to the engine manufacturer's manual.
PROPERTY DAMAGE RISK!
Mixing coolants can lead to clogging the cooler's duct and seriously
damage the engine.
Do not mix different coolants.

NOTICE
The cooling system is filled at the factory with a prepared mixture of water
and Caterpillar Extended Life Coolant. Cat ELC is a red ethylene glycol
based fluid. Only this same Cat ELC fluid or distilled water can be added to
the cooling system.

section_title: Principle of down the hole drilling (DTH)
text:
Principle of down the hole drilling (DTH)
7.1.1
Percussion
Percussion power is a combination of impact
energy and impact frequency. This power is di-
rectly controlled by the level of flushing (per-
cussion) pressure.
The percussion power used depends on the
ground being drilled. If the rock is soft, use
pressure in the low end of the range. If the
rock is hard, use pressure from the upper end
of the range.
The flushing (percussion) pressure setting is
usually a compromise between penetration
rate and equipment durability.
260 (348)

metadata.parent_section: Principle of down the hole drilling (DTH)

text:
DI450-DI560 TIER4
7.1.2
Feed
The purpose of feed is to keep the drill bit in
tight contact with the rock.
When percussion pressure is increased, feed
pressure should also be increased. The cor-
rect feed force depends on percussion pres-
sure, rock type, hole depth, and the size and
type of the drilling equipment.
Fractured rock should be drilled using low per-
cussion and feed pressure.
Optimal feed pressure can be obtained by
“looking” and “listening”.
Look for:
• smooth forward rotary drive movement
• smooth rotation of the drill steel
• tight drill pipe connections
• a steady penetration rate.
Listen for:
• a “solid” percussion sound (not a rattling or
vibrating sound).
Increasing feed force does not increase pene-
tration rate - it will only increase bit wear and
cause hole deviation, above normal rotation
pressures and bent drill pipes.
Too little feed force results in the bit not being
in firm contact with the rock. If this is allowed
to happen, the percussion energy will rebound
back up the drill string instead of going into the
rock, causing damage to the drill string, the
DTH hammer and the feed carriage.
261 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
7.1.3
Rotation
The main function of rotation in percussive
drilling is to turn the drill bit to a new position
after each blow from the piston. Optimum rota-
tion speed depends on bit geometry, bit size
and rock drillability (hardness, abrasiveness,
etc.) and the other main functions of drilling.
A too low rotation speed results in energy loss-
es due to recutting of the rock chips and low
penetration rates. The cuttings come out of the
drilled hole as grindings rather than chips.
A too high rotation speed results in excessive
bit wear because the rock is broken by rotation
rather than percussion. A higher than required
drill bit rotation speed also leads to excessive-
ly tightened pipe connections, causing uncou-
pling problems.
DTH hammers must always be rotated to the
right (clockwise) during drilling, since the
equipment, including the chuck and the top
sub, has right-handed threads.
262 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
7.1.4
Flushing
The purpose of flushing is to remove rock cut-
tings from the drilled hole. After passing the
percussion unit, pressurized air is released in-
to the bottom of the hole through the flushing
holes in the drill bit. The rock cuttings mix with
the flushing air and they are blown out of the
drill hole through the space between the drill
string and the hole wall. This annulus area is
very small and therefore the bailing velocity is
high, which results in the efficient removal of
cuttings even from the deepest holes.
Poor flushing leads to low penetration rate (in-
creased recutting), decreased drill string life,
increased bit wear and an increased possibility
of jamming.
263 (348)

Drilling tools
Floating spool bottom adapter
The feed force and rotation torque of the rotation
drive are transferred to the drilling tools through
the bottom adapter. Floating percussion ensures
smooth threading.
Top adapters
A top adapter equipped with male or female
threads connects the rotation head to the drill
pipe and acts as a wear part.
Pipe equipment
The drill pipes have tapered API threads: a male
thread in one end and a female thread in the
other.
Shock absorber
In some cases, shock absorbers can help to de-
crease the effects of vibration in difficult drilling
conditions.
Air-bypass bottom adapter
The air-bypass bottom adapters are used in diffi-
cult drilling conditions when extra flushing is de-
sired – for example, if a large amount of water
flows into the hole, the difference between the
drill bit and pipe diameter is high, or the penetra-
tion rate is exceptionally high.
264 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
Bottom adapters
Bottom adapters are required when the top
threading of the hammer differs from that of the
drill pipe thread.
DTH hammers
The top adapter connects the hammer to the drill
pipe or bottom adapter. The chuck is threaded to
the front end of the hammer cylinder. The
splined joint between the adapter and drill bit
shank transfers the rotation to the drill bit.
Drill bits
The button bit is the most common drill bit type.
It has a good penetration rate, and it is durable
and easy to sharpen. There are various types of
button drill bits – for example, flat (1), convex
(2), and concave (3) – each suited to different

DANGER
DRILL RIG TIPPING HAZARD!
Risk of death or serious injury.
Never exceed the specified maximum inclination angles.
If the terrain inclination is more than 20°, a winch must be used.
When tramming on a slippery surface such as ice or smooth rock,
the safe inclination angle is noticeably smaller than on non-slippery
surfaces.
Before tramming the drill rig onto a transport platform or ship, move
the boom and feed to the tramming position.
RISK OF DAMAGING THE DRILL RIG!
Take the rig dimensions into consideration when planning the
transport route.

NOTICE
• Run the rotation head to its front position against the front endstop.
• When tramming the rig onto or from a transport platform, use suitable
ramps. This must always be done on level ground.
• Always use low tramming speed and observe great caution, especially
when leaving the ramps and approaching the platform.
• When tramming on a ramp, the rig must be balanced using the boom and
oscillation movements.
• Lock the oscillation valves before transport.
• Lower the jacks (optional) onto the transport platform before transport.
• Lower the boom as far down as possible. Anchor the boom and feed to
the transport platform before transport.
• Fasten the drill rig to the transport platform with straps and chains so that
it cannot move during transport.
Transportation support in the transport position

DI450-DI560 TIER4
The weight of the rig with accessories and drilling equipment attached is 26
000 kg. Before lifting, check the weight of the rig in question from the rig
specification card delivered with the rig.
1. Turn the boom and the feed to their extreme right position and tram the
feed to the rear position using the feed extension.
2. Turn off the diesel engine and turn the ignition key to position 0. Wait until
the yellow indicator light of the main switch goes off and turn the main
switch to position OFF.
3. Attach the lifting equipment carefully to the rig’s lifting points. The rig’s
lifting points are located on the right-hand side of the cabin on the carrier
and boom.
4. Lift the rig slowly and make sure that it is balanced.
5. Move the hanging rig with slow and steady movements. Do not lift the rig
any higher than you need to.
6. When lowering the rig, make sure that you can see the landing point at
all times. If necessary, ask somebody to check the other side of the rig.
7. Once the rig has been lowered to the ground, detach the lifting
equipment.
276 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
7.11.1
Load lifting
Most countries have regulations concerning lifting, lifting wires, and lifting
devices. These local safety regulations must always be followed.
• Use only an appropriate lifting device type with sufficient lifting capacity.
Neither the rig nor its components may be lifted with devices other than
those designed for lifting purposes. Do not use, for instance, a loader for
lifting.
• The weight of the load must be known, and the capacity of the lifting
device, as specified by the manufacturer, must not be exceeded.
• Lifting routes should be chosen so that
- the load is not lifted over people
- the load is not lifted over areas where people may be present
• Before lifting, check that the lifting device is in good condition.
• The lifting wires and chains must be checked regularly. Discarded wires
must be marked clearly and removed from use immediately.
• The lifting points of drilling rigs are marked with adhesive labels as
shown below. Only lift the rig from these points.
• Make sure that the load is properly attached and balanced. To check the
fastening and balance, lift the load by just a few centimeters initially. Do

fastening and balance, lift the load by just a few centimeters initially. Do
not continue lifting until you are certain that the fastening and balance are
good.
• Lifting wires consisting of many parts may not be twisted. Lifting ropes
must be fastened according to the manufacturer's instructions.
Incorrect
Correct
• Never loop the lifting wire around the load. Always use a proper lifting
strap or chain.
277 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
Incorrect
Correct
• Do not lower the load more than is necessary. At least two loops of wire
must be left on the reel. This way, you can make sure that the weight of
the load is evenly distributed between the wire and its fixing point on the
reel.
• Never stand or work under a hanging load.
• Check the capacity and length of the wire whenever the wire or mast
length is altered.
• Do not estimate lifting capacity on the basis of stability.
• Never lift a load over people.
• Take care that the load does not bump into anything.
• When lifting personnel, only use lifting devices that are purpose-built for
lifting people. Never lift people with lifting devices that are not designed
for that purpose. Drilling rig booms are not designed for lifting people!
• It is strictly forbidden to carry people on the load.

A crane and suitable lifting equipment for the pipes are required for pipe
loading. Alternatively, you can use a bracket intended for the storage of
pipes and load the pipes to the cassette from this bracket.
1. Tram the drill rig alongside the pipes to be loaded.
2. Lift the pipe to be loaded with the crane so that it is at an approximate
angle of 45 degrees. (You can also use the bracket.)
3. Run the feed to a corresponding angle with the pipes and open the
break-out table holding and swing jaws and the guide jaws.
4. Draw the pipe to be loaded from the lower end of the feed through the
suction head and break-out table jaws to the feed. (Take the pipe as far
as necessary so that the rotation head can be threaded onto the pipe.)
5. Close the break-out table holding jaws.
6. Thread the rotation head onto the pipe.
7. Remove the lifting equipment from the pipe.
8. Load the pipe into the cassette. For further instructions, refer to the
section Loading the drill pipes into the cassette.
9. If necessary, load the next pipe as described above.

Adjusting the safety wire
During safety wire adjustment, the engine must be stopped. The control
system must be running.
1. Adjust the wire pre-tension with adjustment screw A so that the edge of
attachment link B of the lower end of the wire is at the groove on the
switch lever.
A
B
C

2. Pull the wire.
The control system display shows a message indicating that the safety
wire has been activated.
3. Reset the safety wire switch by pressing button C on the switch.
The switch is reset and the message on the control system display is
cleared.
An incorrectly adjusted safety wire may cause the safety wire to be
activated during tramming or drilling.

Water injection system (option)
7.15.1
Filling the water tank
1. Turn off the engine.
2

2. Remove the claw-coupling plug (1).
3. Connect the water-filling hose to the claw coupling.
4. Turn the valve (2) handle up.
5. Fill the water tank.
Note!
Use tap water or filtered water ( < 177 microns ).
Monitor the filling of the water tank from the control system display.
6. After the tank is full, detach the water-filling hose, turn the valve (2)
handle to its middle position, and plug the claw coupling.
283 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
7.15.2
Water tank draining
1. Turn off the diesel engine. Wait until the yellow indicator light of the main
switch goes off and turn the main switch to position OFF.
2. Make sure that the device cannot be accidentally started.
3. Detach the protective plate from under the water tank.
4. Place a receptacle under the water-tank drain plug.
5. Open the water-tank drain plug.
6. When the water tank is empty, turn the drain plug back into place, and
reinstall the protective plate under the tank.
7.15.3
Drying sequence
When the operator stops the water injection with the switch of the side pillar
panel, the automatic drying sequence starts. Hoses and pipes are dried
with pressurized air.

Overriding the sensors
7.21.1
By-passing the armrest safety switch
RISK OF INJURY!
Can cause death or serious injury.
Observe extreme caution when using the drill rig with a safety switch
by-passed. The safety switches on the armrests may be by-passed
only temporarily in exceptional situations, such as if the safety
switch has failed.
Only by-pass the armrest panel that has the broken safety switch.
1. Press both side buttons on the smaller armrest panel joystick
simultaneously, for five seconds.
The safety switch is now by-passed.
7.21.2
By-passing the rear jack position sensor
Use the rear jack position sensor by-pass only to enable the drill rig to be
trammed to a safe place for replacing the broken sensor.
1. Raise the rear jack.
2. Go outside the drill rig to make sure that the rear jack is up.
3. Press the rear-jack up button and keep it depressed.
290 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
4. After pressing the button for five seconds, you can start tramming the drill
rig.
Keep the button depressed during the entire tramming distance. If you lift
the button during the tramming, center the tramming joystick and return
to step 3.

Central lubrication (optional)
7.22.1
Function
The MultiLube system is a central lubrication system in which the pumping
unit (1) pumps the lubricant through piping (2) to the dosers (3). The dosers
feed the pre-selected amount of lubricant to the lubrication points. The
operation of the system is controlled and monitored with a control unit (4).
The control unit controls the system by automatically starting the process at
an interval chosen by the user and monitors the pressurisation of the
system and the amount of lubricant. If the pressure does not reach a set
level within the set maximum pressurisation time, or if the amount of
lubricant drops to the lower limit, the control unit indicates a malfunction
with the warning lights on the button panel (5) (rigs with a cabin) or via the
user interface (6) (rigs without a cabin).

7.22.2
Pumping unit
Location of the pumping unit
The central lubrication pumping unit is located in the rear right-hand corner
of the drill rig.
Operation
When the pressurisation stage begins, the control starts the pump and
opens the line valve. In the pressure switch operation, the pump stops
when the pressure switch closes and restarts when it opens. After the pre-
selected pressurisation time, the control stops the pump and the pressure
from the line is discharged into the lubricant reservoir.
If the lubricant level drops to the low limit during pumping, the reservoir’s
low level switch sends an alarm signal to the control and pumping is
stopped. The alarm status disappears when the lubricant reservoir is filled
and the alarm is reset.
292 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
Filling the lubricant reservoir of the pumping unit
The lubricant reservoir of the pumping unit is filled through the filling
connector equipped with a filter.
1. Ensure that the area surrounding the pumping unit is clean. Impurities in
the system prevent its trouble-free operation and cause damage when
they reach the lubrication point.
2. Remove the lid from the lubricant container (1) and press the follower
plate (2) tightly to the container on top of the lubricant. The follower plate
is not used with fluid lubricants, as it would not float on the surface of
such lubricants.
3. Place the container lid (3) on top of the lubricant container. Fasten the
container lid to the container with the wing screws (4).
4. Insert the pump (5) through the container lid into the follower plate’s
central unit (6).
5. Connect the lubricant hose (7) to the pump.
6. Fill the lubricant hose by pumping by hand.
7. Connect the quick connector (8) to the lubricant hose.
8. Remove the protecting cap (14) of the filling device quick connector.
9. Remove the protecting cap (13) of the pumping unit filling connector.

8. Remove the protecting cap (14) of the filling device quick connector.
9. Remove the protecting cap (13) of the pumping unit filling connector.
10. Connect the quick connector to the pumping unit filling connector (9).
11. Fill the lubricant reservoir of the pumping unit by pumping slowly by
hand.
12. The filling of the lubricant reservoir can be monitored with the level
indicator (10). To prevent overfilling, the pumping unit is equipped with a
safety valve (11).
13. Turn the pump handle (12) to a vertical orientation to discharge the
pressure so it passes to the lubricant container.
14. Disconnect the quick connector (8) from the pumping unit filling
connector (9).
15. Fasten the protecting cap (13) of the pumping unit filling connector.
16. Fasten the protecting cap (14) of the filling device quick connector.

DI450-DI560 TIER4
Interval time counter
In normal mode, the display shows the time elapsed, in hours and minutes,
since the previous pressurisation. The green indicator light for line 1 or 2
indicates the line that was pressurised last.
Time elapsed in the cycle is always counted except when
• the system is in alarm mode or receives no power
• the interlock switch is closed
• the system is shut down (maximum pressurisation time = 0).
Pressurisation
The pressurisation time is displayed in seconds. The green indicator light
for line 1 or 2 indicates the line to be pressurised. Green indicator light P
indicates that pressurisation is in progress.
The pressurisation can be stopped with the SET button.
Extra lubrication cycle
An extra lubrication cycle can be started if the system is not in alarm mode
or shut down, or if pressurising is not in progress.
The extra lubrication cycle can be started from normal mode with the SET
button when the display is showing the time elapsed since the previous
pressurisation.
In single-line systems, the line is pressurised once.
The extra lubrication can be stopped with the SET button.

In pressure sensor operation, the line pressure discharge is monitored. A
new pressurisation is started only when the pressure in the line has
decreased below the set limit.
The stage code dC is displayed while the pressure is being discharged.

text:
DI450-DI560 TIER4
Alarms
The system is in alarm mode if the red indicator light for line 1 , line 2 , or
pressurisation ( P ) is flashing.
Low level alarm
The system triggers the low level alarm, stops the pressurisation, and
switches to alarm mode if the lubricant reservoir is empty - i.e., the
reservoir’s low level switch is closed. At this point, code AGr flashes on the
display and red pressurisation indicator light P flashes. The pressurisation
starts again for the same line when the low limit switch has opened and the
alarm has been reset using the SET button.
Pressure alarm
The system triggers the pressure alarm and switches to alarm mode if the
line pressure has not decreased sufficiently by the time pressurising starts,
or if the line pressure has not increased sufficiently during the
pressurisation.
High pressure alarm
If the line pressure has not decreased sufficiently when pressurising starts,
the code AHP flashes on the display and the red indicator light for the line 1
causing the alarm flashes.
In the pressure sensor operation , the alarm is triggered if the line
pressure is over the selected low limit when the pressurisation starts. The

In the pressure sensor operation , the alarm is triggered if the line
pressure is over the selected low limit when the pressurisation starts. The
pressurisation starts again for the same line when the pressure has
decreased below the low limit and the alarm has been reset using the SET
button.
Low pressure alarm
If the line pressure does not increase sufficiently during the pressurising,
the code ALP flashes on the display and the red indicator light for the line 1
causing the alarm flashes.
In pressure sensor operation , the alarm is triggered if the line pressure
does not reach the set limit value within the set maximum pressurisation
time. The pressurisation starts again for the same line when the alarm has
been reset using the SET button.
Settings
The set values - for example, lubrication cycle and maximum pressurisation
time - are setpoint values for the lubrication system.
All settings are password-protected.
299 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4

2. Press the SET button.
The display shows the code PPP

Note!
The password for the settings is 103
4. Press the
button to move to the next digit.
5. After entering all three digits of the password, press the SET button.
If the correct password is entered, the code ACC is displayed and the
settings can be changed for the next 10 minutes.
If the password is not entered correctly, the code Err is displayed and the
display returns to normal mode.

1. Press the

The lubrication cycle setting is displayed in hours and minutes. Hours and

Water heater (optional)
7.23.1
General
The water heater operates only when the rig’s main switch is in the “ON”
position (power on).
The rig’s main switch may not be switched off when the heater is
activated or in use. The rig’s main switch turns off the power to the
whole electrical system; the heater and timer are switched off too.
When the rig’s main switch has just been turned on, all indicators on the
display flash – the timer must be re-programmed. In this condition, the
heater cannot be used.
When the ignition key is in the running position (power on), the display
show the day and time. When the power is switched off, the clock
disappears from the display within 15 seconds.
301 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
7.23.2
Description of operation
Switching on
When the heater is switched on using the switch or the timer, the heating
symbol appears on the display.
The heater starts according to a preset program, whereby the water pump
and the combustion air fan start up first. The metering pump starts fuel feed
somewhat delayed. The glow phase of the glow plugs begins
simultaneously with the distribution of the combustion air. The glow plugs
are switched off after a stable flame has formed in the combustion
chamber.
Heating
At the initial start the heater is operated with the "POWER" (12000 W)
stage until the water temperature exceeds the "POWER" / "HIGH"
changeover treshold.
Then, depending on the heat requirement, the heater switches to one of the
following stages:
• HIGH (9500 W)
• MEDIUM 1 (5000 W)
• MEDIUM 2 (3500 W)
• MEDIUM 3 (1500 W)
• LOW (1200 W)
• OFF
If the heating requirement in the "LOW" stage is so small that the coolant
temperature reaches 85°C, the heater switches from "LOW" to "OFF". This
is followed by the after-run with additional after glowing of the glow plugs.
The after-run lasts approx. 180 seconds. The water pump stays on.

is followed by the after-run with additional after glowing of the glow plugs.
The after-run lasts approx. 180 seconds. The water pump stays on.
After the coolant has cooled down to approx. 70°C the heater starts in the
"MEDIUM 1" stage.
The coolant temperature sensor switches the rig's fan on when the coolant
temperature reaches approx. 55°C. The fan is turned off if the coolant
temperature drops down to approx. 40°C.
Switching off
On being switched off the heater starts the after-run of 180 seconds. During
the after-run both of the glow plugs also after glow.
Heating at high altitudes
When the heater is used over 3500 m above sea level, the heater adjusts
the fuel feed because of the lower air density. The heater recognizes the
change in the air density and adjusts the air/fuel mixture to accommodate
to the changed conditions. At high altitudes the maximum output power of
the heater is 8500 W.
302 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
7.23.3
Control and safety equipment
The heater is equipped with the following control and safety devices:
• If the heater does not ignite within 74 seconds after starting the fuel
pump, the start is repeated. If the heater still does not ignite after another
65 seconds, the heater is switched off. After an unacceptable number of
failed start attempts, the controller is locked.
• If the flame goes off by itself during operation, the heater is restarted. If
the heater does not ignite within 74 seconds after the fuel pump has
started again, the heater is switched off. This status can be remedied by
briefly switching off and on again.
• In the case of overheating (e.g. lack of coolant, poorly vented coolant
circuit), the overheating sensor triggers, the fuel supply is interrupted,
and the heater switches off. Once the cause of overheating has been
eliminated, the heater can be re-started by switching off and on again (on
a condition that the heater has cooled down again sufficiently and coolant
temperature is below 70°C). After the heater has been switched off for
overheating an unacceptable number of times, the controller is locked.

temperature is below 70°C). After the heater has been switched off for
overheating an unacceptable number of times, the controller is locked.
The controller can be enabled again and the faults read off:
- using the module timer / EasyStart T
- using the radio remote control TP5 / EasyStart T / EasyStart R+
- by connecting up a diagnosis unit
- using the diagnosis tool EDITH.
For operation and fault list, please refer to the enclosed operating
instructions or the troubleshooting and repair instructions for the heater.
• The heater is switched off if the upper or lower voltage limit is reached.
• The heater does not start up if the electric cable to the metering pump is
interrupted.
• If one of the two glow plugs is defective the start sequence takes place
with one glow plug only.
• The speed of the fan motor is monitored continuously. If the fan motor
does not start up, or if the speed differs by over 12,5 % from the desired
speed a safety lockout (shutdown on faults) takes place after 60
seconds.
• The water pump's function is continuously monitored.
303 (348)

Tramming instructions
7.24.1
Switching on the main power and opening the door
1. Switch on the main power by turning the switch to the ON position.
2. Open the cabin door.
304 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
7.24.2
Checking the emergency stops
1. Make sure that none of the emergency stops have been pressed.
Placement of the emergency stops:
• on the right side of the rig (under the cover)
• at the back left corner of the rig
• on the front left side of the rig
• inside the cabin, on the side pillar panel
305 (348)

DANGER
MOBILE MACHINERY HAZARD!
Unexpected rig movements will cause death or severe injury.
Before start-up, you must always check that there are no
unauthorized persons within the danger areas of the engine or the
drilling rig and that the control devices are not switched to the
operating position.
EXPLOSION HAZARD!
Explosion could cause death or severe injury.
The use of ether or other aerosol-type system-external substances
for starting is prohibited. Use of such substances can cause
accident or engine failure.
RISK OF DAMAGING THE RIG!
Using a booster-type starting aid device for start-up assistance is
prohibited. A booster-type starting aid device increases the voltage
of the electrical system. Using higher voltage damages the electrical
system. If there is a need for a starting aid device, use batteries of
the same voltage.
The engine cannot be started by towing.

RIG TIPPING HAZARD!
Causes death or serious injury.
Never exceed the specified maximum inclination angles.
When tramming on a slippery surface such as ice or smooth rock,
the safe inclination angle is noticeably smaller than on non-slippery

RISK OF DAMAGING THE RIG!
Take the rig dimensions into consideration when planning the
transport route.

NOTICE
• Run the rotation head to its front position against the front endstop.
• When tramming the rig onto or from a transport platform, use suitable
ramps. This must always be done on level ground.
• Always use low tramming speed and observe great caution, especially
when leaving the ramps and approaching the platform.
• When tramming on a ramp, the rig must be balanced using the boom and
oscillation movements.
• Lock the oscillation valves before transport.
• Lower the jacks (optional) onto the transport platform before transport.
• Lower the boom as far down as possible. Anchor the boom and feed to
the transport platform before transport.
• Fasten the rig to the platform with straps and chains so that it cannot
move during transport.

7.24.9
Stopping the engine
RISK OF DAMAGING THE RIG!
Stopping the engine in an uncontrolled manner will cause error
messages to be stored in the ECM. This may, in turn, void the
warranty of the aftertreatment system.
The emergency stop buttons must only be used in an emergency
situation. Never stop the diesel engine with the emergency stop
buttons or main power switch during normal operation.

DI450-DI560 TIER4
1. Stop the diesel engine by pressing the stop button (1) or by turning the
ignition key (3) to position 0. The engine will stop after a while (at
maximum, 30 minutes). During this time the pneumatic system pressure
is decreased and the diesel engine is cooled down before the engine is
stopped.
3
2
1
The phases in the engine stop sequence are shown on the control
system display.
After the diesel engine has stopped, the DEF fluid is drained from the
system to the DEF tank in order to prevent freezing. A pop-up window
showing the remaining time is displayed on the control system display.
324 (348)

DI450-DI560 TIER4
7.24.10
Switching off the main power
1. Turn the ignition key (3) to position 0 to turn off the control current.
2. Wait until the yellow indicator light of the main switch goes off and turn off
the main power by turning the switch to position OFF. This may take up
to 30 minutes.

routes.
Never go beneath a hanging load or try to guide a hanging load by
hand.
